era_anchor.definition: chase needing >= 9 RPO with ~60 balls left (balls_left in 57..63)

evidence.note: One minimum-evidence rule for BOTH leagues (§0.1). A win cell built on fewer than 12 real chases, or a runs cell on fewer than 15 real innings, is thin ground. The WPL renders those as a hole ('not enough WPL cricket yet'); IPL free-play shows the number but marks it as filled in by rule, not counted, and hatches its grid square. The default spot and mainstream chases are well above the threshold, so the toy's common path shows real numbers.

calibration.text: Every number here is a lookup, not a guess. We scored each of 144,136 real chase moments with the grid and checked it against what actually happened. Across the board the grid was off by about 1.1 points of win probability, and never more than 3.4 points in any well-stocked band.

evidence_mask.text: The WPL is only four seasons old, so many spots have too few games to call honestly. We hatch any win-grid spot built on fewer than 12 chases and any runs-grid spot built on fewer than 15 innings, and label them plainly: not enough WPL cricket yet.

wickets_early_rate_late.text: Early on, wickets in hand are everything and the rate is soft. With 16 overs left and 11 an over to get, ten wickets in hand instead of five lifts your odds from 11% to 46%. With two overs left it flips: the same ten wickets buy you nothing, 61% either way, because there is no time to use them. Late, only the rate matters.

intro.orient: Set the chase with three sliders: how many overs are left, how many wickets are still standing, and how many runs the batting side needs.
intro.reveal: Two meters answer at once. One is how often teams win from a spot like this. The other is how many runs they usually still get. Every reading is a real count from 1,331 matches, not a guess.

runs.gloss: expected runs to come from here
runs.label: Runs a team usually still gets

win.gloss: out of every 100 chases from a spot like this
win.label: How often teams win from here

note: Two dials on one lookup. The win meter reads engine #3's chase-win grid; the runs meter reads engine #2's run-expectancy surface, re-indexed to the same (overs_left, wickets_in_hand) coordinate. No live model. The widget interpolates and looks up, never fits.

copy.orient: Chennai chasing 206, 2018, Dhoni set at the crease. 5 overs left, 6 wickets in hand, still 71 to get.
copy.reveal: From a spot this steep teams usually make about 50 more runs and win about 30 in 100. Dhoni got them there anyway.

copy.orient: One exact square, back in 2010. Ten overs left, three down, needing 100. That is 10 an over.
copy.reveal: From this one spot teams won about 21 in 100 and usually made about 88 more. Now press the same chase in 2025 and watch only the year change.

copy.orient: The same one square, now in 2025. Nothing on the sliders moved. Ten overs left, three down, needing 100, still 10 an over.
copy.reveal: From this exact spot teams now win about 34 in 100 and make about 97 more. Same square, later era, and the chase got kinder.

index.rrr_bucket: first i where runs_needed*6/(overs_left*6) < rrr_edges[i], else 9

title: The Net Session

wpl.note: Flip the toggle to the WPL. It is four seasons old, not nineteen, so many spots have too few games to read. Those render hatched: not enough WPL cricket yet. A young league, told honestly, not a league behind.
